He was one of many figures in the American occult scene of the time to be strongly influenced by Mesmerism, and proceeded to develop an extensive body of occult philosophy and practice based partly on Mesmer's teachings and partly on his own visionary experiences. Much of his work was picked up and elaborated by the Spiritualists of a later generation, and remained standard in American occult circles until the heyday of Theosophy.

Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though I'm going to have somewhat limited email access tomorrow and it may be Tuesday or even Wednesday before I get to them all. I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

replies.title: Re: I'd like to see Manly Wade Wellman or John Hohman
replies.content: John Hohman would certainly be an option. I wasn't aware that Wellman was a practicing occultist, though he was a helluva writer -- the Silver John story "The Desrick on Yandro" was the first thing of his I read, and it remains a favorite tale of mine. If you've got sources that document that he was a practitioner, I'd love to see them! As for the Petit Albert -- by that you presumably mean the book, or did you have the author in mind?

replies.title: Re: I'd like to see Manly Wade Wellman or John Hohman
replies.content: According to his books Wellman was a believer, if not a practitioner. I was thinking of the book Petit Albert, not so much Albertus Magnus, but either would be great.

replies.title: Re: I'd like to see Manly Wade Wellman or John Hohman
replies.content:
To the best of my knowledge, Wellman wasn't an occultist or esotericist at all (no more than H. P. Lovecraft), though (like Lovecraft) his stories are full of occult lore. Most of what Wellman knew appears to have come to him from his good friend, the folklorist Vance Randolph, author of a fine book on Ozark magic and magical folklore; but Wellman doesn't appear to have been anywhere as knowledgeable as Randolph was about folkloric magic. He did, however, have a good grasp of John Hohman's spellbook, "The Long Lost Friend."

"Petit Albert" and "Grand Albert" are not people at all, but the abbreviated titles of two popular French spellbooks, printed and sold in countless editions from at least the 1700s down to the present day. They are translations of two popular Medieval books of occult lore, printed and reprinted in Latin from the late 1400s well into the 1500s; these books were ascribed to the famous Medieval scholar, Albertus Magnus, but do not seem to have been by him at all. Their true author remains unknown. (Jung knew one of these two works, and possibly both of them, from a very early Latin edition in the Zürich Library; he has a longish quote from the preface to one of the two in his book, "Synchronicity: An Acausal Connecting Principle.")

replies.title: Re: I'd like to see Manly Wade Wellman or John Hohman
replies.content: PS  Wellman does seem to have been a fairly devout Christian, again to judge by his stories.  He shared that religion's fear of magic and witchcraft, but was quite comfortable with some of the more "magical" aspects (as we might think of them) used by Christians to defend themselves against witchcraft and malevolent magic.

comments.title: sympathetic ancestors
comments.content:
Greetings John.

My questions are to do with paying respects to, and requesting help from, one's ancestors.

My impression from reading this and your other page is that departed people retain an interest and influence in/on their descendants' affairs for a certain time and may evolve into the position of gods to those descendants.

Now, to the extent I have understood this correctly, could you outline your understanding of what bearing one's previous relations with such people, and their erstwhile personalities, have on subsequent interactions?

For instance, I knew 3 of my grandparents. From my sense of their attitudes and values, I'd predict that 2 of them would disapprove of me and my present activities and hence not be interested in helping my magical and spiritual development. The third, though, would likely be sympathetic. The one I never knew I infer from the family stories I would not agree with over much.

But I am concerned about raising resentment or jealousy from the others if I pray to only one. (Yes, they were rather thin-skinned types in life.) Further, is there a case for trying out the three I don't expect would wish to help me? And what about approaching the spirits of people who aren't my ancestors but I'd maybe wish had been?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: sympathetic ancestors
replies.content:
Okay, to begin with, there's a lot of variability in the different traditions of ancestor reverence. The one I more or less grew up with is the Japanese tradition, in which individual ancestors are revered for 33 years after death; any ancestor who's been dead longer than that is assumed to have gone onto a new incarnation, and receives general offerings directed to the ancestors collectively.

If your grandparents have been dead less than 33 years, according to this tradition, you can pray to them individually. If they've been dead longer than that, simply include them in prayers to your ancestors collectively. Do pray to all of them, whether they would be sympathetic to you or not -- but you can choose what you say to each of them on the basis of their personalities in life, i.e., ask for general blessings from the less sympathetic ones and for help on specific projects for the more sympathetic ones.

replies.title: Re: sympathetic ancestors
replies.content:
Wow, brilliant! This makes much sense. Thank you for the clarification.

As so often happens, the timing of something I am thinking about is spookily apropos. Remember last year I mentioned the orgonite donors... it turns out that the instigator of that project, Don Croft, was dying pretty much at the time I asked you about them.
The grandparents whose lives overlapped mine all died between 1985 and 1989. The sympathetic grandmother has just passed out of the 33-year frame!; her husband's 33rd anniversary is this month; with the other grandparent (the one I had most contact with) there are exactly 3 years left for specific prayers, in this tradition. I consider this very meaningful.

With gratitude,

kallianeira.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I think you've misunderstood my comments, which were specific to a particular kind of talismanic magic. As Regardie pointed out in one of his books, magic is to some extent the art of horoscope repair; you can't change your horoscope, but you can certainly change the level of consciousness with which you respond to the factors in your natal chart, and strengthen other factors to compensate for whatever weaknesses you may have. It's one thing to go through a Saturn return blindly, getting clobbered by whatever Saturn throws at you, and quite another to do it when you're conscious of the whole process, comfortable with Saturnine energies, and able to balance out Saturn's vagaries with other planetary energies so that you minimize the trauma and maximize the stabilizing and strengthening effects of Saturn's presence.

As for the LBRP, it primarily works on the astral levels of being, and so I recommend the use of other protective methods if you're dealing with magical attack on the etheric levels -- for example, a lot of hoodoo works on the etheric plane, and while a banishing ritual will do you some good against a foot track spell, say, a little salt and hot pepper sprinkled under the insole of each shoe is also worth doing!

replies.title: Re: Candle flame
replies.content:
Fire is an etheric as well as a physical phenomenon, in a flame, chemical bonds (which are physical) are broken and their energy transformed into light (which is etheric). A flame thus works well any time you want to direct your intention up the planes so that it has magical effect.

You can do a quite decent job of the R+C ritual tracing the lines with your hand, the same way you'd do a Pentagram ritual; it takes a little more effort to get the same intensity of effect, but that's the ony issue. If you want to crank the intensity up several notches, make and consecrate a wand specifically for the R+C ritual; use solar symbolism and anoint it with attar of roses for best effect.

comments.title: Birds - updated
comments.content:
I got into a daily practice of the Sphere of Protection about two months ago after asking you about its impact on wild birds. Since then, I've put a birdbath in my yard and several species are daily visitors including a few that I've never seen before. Birds generally seem to be completely unafraid of me now, and a few will regularly perch on branches overhead while I practice the S of P.

Thank you for your help. Are there any sources of Druid lore about birds that you would recommend to a beginner, and how do I communicate with them?

replies.title: Re: Birds - updated
replies.content:
Excellent! That's a very good sign.

I don't know of a good source of Druid lore about birds -- remember that Druidry has had to be reinvented from the ground up, and a lot of other things have had to be put together over the last three hundred years; we're about to the point where birdlore and animal lore are relevant (thus Philip Carr-Gomm's very capably done The Druid Animal Oracle, a solid step in that direction). I'd encourage you to start by combining personal observation with some serious reading in bird biology and behavior; though he's unfashionable these days, the Austrian scientist Konrad Lorenz is worth reading along these lines. As you do this, see what the birds communicate to you -- they're quite clever enough to make their wishes and concerns known to you.

comments.title: Intense Training
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I am a young occultist who only recently started to practice ritual magic. However, I would like to go above and beyond what an average magician does in his training, I want to reach the summit of magical attainment and push the boundaries of what’s considered possible, to conquer the world and remake it in my image. There has to be some intense but reasonable training course which doesn’t end with me being in a madhouse or in an early grave.

You once said you’d prefer to see an excessively ambitious pupil over a sycophant, so there you go.

With the SoP as my basic ritual what would my training regimen for the next 10 years look like?

Yours truly,
Lord Moldywort ;-)

replies.title: Re: Intense Training
replies.content:
I'm glad to hear it. The old Rosicrucian motto Festina lente -- literally, "hurry slowly" -- is worth keeping in mind; trying to rush your practices won't get you where you want to go. You need to start by building a really solid foundation.

That means daily practice of the SoP, meditation, and divination, with an emphasis on meditation -- 30 minutes a day of actual meditation should be your minimum for the first two years, working up to an hour a day thereafter. Once you've got the basic method of the SoP down cold, the expansions given in my book The Druid Magic Handbook are your next step, and the steps after that are in a forthcoming book of mine, The Dolmen Arch. Meanwhile get a solid background in natural magic -- the magical properties of herbs, stones, etc. -- and do a lot of practical work with these, and master at least two systems of divination inside and out, so your readings are precise and stone cold accurate. Once you've done that -- not before -- get some good books on spagyrics (herbal alchemy) and get to work on that; spagyric work is a great way to accelerate your inner development, once you've got the basics of magic down.

That's the practical work, and if you do it with sufficient intensity it'll take you ten years of hard work. You also need to put serious time into study. That means mythology and folklore, the raw material from which magic springs; the history of magic, so you know what your roots are and can make sense of older texts and traditions; the literature of magic itself, from the Graeco-Egyptian magical papyri straight on through the latest postmodern avant-garde stuff; and at least some grounding in philosophy as well. To achieve what you want to achieve, you're going to have to have a really solid intellectual understanding of magic as well as a really solid practical grasp of it; it's going to take you roughly the amount of work you'd expect to put in to earn a B.A., M.A., and Ph.D. at a really good university.

By the time you finish that, you'll know enough to be able to chart your course for the next ten years -- because the work doesn't finish there, of course. The Path stands open before you. Do you have what it takes to do it? Only you can answer that.

--Sidney

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I've encountered different opinions regarding this. Some people get good results by doing planetary charity with all seven planets each week; others have more success focusing on one at a time. You may need to experiment. Wearing planetary colors, btw, isn't limited to the Mughal court -- I have a friend who has polo shirts in the seven planetary colors and wears them on appropriate days, and makes simple offerings to the planetary gods (incense and prayer) on the days as well. The results are quite good.

2) According to Lilly, Jupiter rules students in universities as well as their teachers, so a donation to a scholarship fund would be entirely appropriate. You could also donate to a legal defense fund, since judges and justice are Jovial correspondences.

3) Oh, he'll transcend the terrestrial realm all right; back in the day, he would have had the assistance of the local magistrates and, depending on jurisdiction, an executioner's sword or a stake and a load of dry wood. One thing a lot of modern students of old magical lore don't realize is that a lot of the old books are boobytrapped six ways from Sunday to get the clueless to remove themselves. There are alchemical recipes that produce high explosives and then tell you to pound them up in a mortar; there are recipes for potions to protect you from spirits that have nice high doses of cyanide and will drop you stone cold dead within a minute or so; and there are rituals involving public actions that will by definition get you hauled before a magistrate and, back in the day, put to death. (That's as true of Islamic law as Christian, of course; sihr, evil magic, ranks among the highest religious crimes under shari'a law and is punishable by death.

If your friend goes ahead with the whole operation he can expect, at best, to spend the rest of his life in a facility for the criminally insane. That's very likely what the ritual is intended to do -- to remove people who are willing to do appalling things from the magical community, and also from the gene pool.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Roger that -- will experiment!

And re my colleague: sorry, I meant he's a fellow historian/philologist trying to make sense of this operation within the context of the larger project of his target thinker, Fakhr al-Din Razi (d. 1210), author of one of the most seminal of all medieval Arabic grimoires, the Hidden Secret (al-Sirr al-maktum). He's certainly not intending to try any of it out! But the thing is, and given that being burnt at the stake was far less likely in the Islamic context than the Christian, these prescriptions really don't seem to be boobytraps in this instance, but are rather presented as an experiential proof for the validity of his larger philosophical program (this in challenge to Avicenna). In such a legitimately philosophical case, it is conceivable that such operations were meant to be imaginal only?

As for sihr, that's an equally polyvalent term, only defined as "black magic" by polemicists -- its proponents, including a majority of early modern Muslim thinkers, refer to it unproblematically and even enthusiastically as just another natural science, and no one, as far as I can tell, was actually put to death for its practice. In other words, the Arabic and especially Persian materials I work with (often written by magistrates!) are rather more straightforward than their Latin cognates, for which boobytrapping makes more social sense. At the same time, the 12th century was fairly bizarre all around...

Transcending the material plane is the work of lifetimes, not of a single set of ritual workings, and very often when you see that kind of bait being dangled for the clueless, the jaws of the trap aren't too difficult to make out.

I recall a fine Chinese example of the species, a recipe for the "golden pill" of immortality that contains very carefully processed and measured doses of mercury and arsenic. All you have to do is compound the pills and start taking them; after a while, your limbs start to tremble, and the book assures you that this is a sign that the ch'i is flowing through your meridians with increased vigor. Then it kills you, of course, and if you've followed instructions your body is so permeated with heavy metals that it doesn't decay right away -- proof that you've achieved immortality in a heavenly realm, providing ample opportunity for the Taoist sage who prescribed the medicine to make his escape...

You play the game and note down every move, including the specific Enochian square every piece lands on in each turn. You then read the sequence of squares as though you were interpreting a Tarot reading, or what have you. The results, if you're any good at the game, tend to be very exact answers to your question. replies.content:
Ha! I like this.

Most games of solitaire can serve as divination systems, whether you play them with an ordinary deck of cards in the ordinary way, or adapt the layout to the greater number of cards in each of the four suits and the added "suit" of trumps in a standard tarot deck. The underlying idea is that whichever cards keep you from winning the game represent the things that keep you from your desire or goal, and that therefore you need to work on.

I happen to be fond of a game of solitaire that my wife and I were taught by her mother while we were all vacationing in Maine, shortly after our marriage. She did not have a name for her game, and I have never been able to find it described in any book of solitaire games, so my wife and I ended up calling it "Lobster" in honor of the tasty food we ate there in Maine. (More generally, it best fits into the "spider" family of solitaire games.)

Ordinary Lobster deals out a tableau of eight columns, row after row for six rows (thus, 48 cards). You put the remaining four cards off to one side face up; they are your reserve. In the first four columns (only), the first four cards are face down--16 face down cards in all. The other 32 cards are face up.

You build down in each suit, from King to Ace, on any card that is at the bottom of a column. As you do this, you can use any face-up card, but you must carry all the cards below it in its column along with it. If you have an empty column, you may put a King there (carrying all the cards with it that happen to be below it in its column). When such movements uncover a face-down card, you will turn that card face-up, so it can come into play.

You can also take cards from your reserve to help you as you build down in each suit. Whenever you have less than four cards in the reserve, you may take cards from the bottom of columns to bring the reserve back up to four cards, thereby making new cards available to build down on.

Technically, you can win the game if you have managed to turn up all 16 of the face-down cards, but a "real" win is if you also have each suit complete in its own column, arranged downward from King to Ace.

To adapt this game to a Tarot deck, deal your cards out row by row in ten columns, not eight. You will end up with seven rows, and your reserve will have eight cards in it. The play is the same, but the trumps work as a fifth "suit" that you hope to build down, from the World to the Fool. As to how many rows have the first *five* (not four) of their cards face down, I have found that four rows face down (20 cards in all) makes a very challenging game for divination, but five rows face down (25 cards) hardly ever moves very close to completion at all, and will leave you with more "obstacles" in your path than a person might conveniently be able to work through.

I hope this is clear enough for you to follow and try out for yourselves.

comments.title: Personal imbalances affecting healing work with others
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

I recently fell off of my bicycle and separated my right shoulder. I'm healing well, and should make a full recovery soon. It got me thinking, though. All of my trauma (a concussion, two toes broken on separate occasions, a fractured wrist, bruised intercostal cartilage, and this shoulder separation) and my major chronic issue (a growth in my larynx which would be benign if not for it's location) are on the right side of my body. (The one exception is a broken toe on the left foot which happened when I was a child). In general my minor injuries are on the right side as well, though these are a bit less one-sided.

There are other factors aside from injury, but in general - and without going into tedious detail - the left side of my body feels a bit "off-line." I am right hand and right leg dominant.

Noticing this, I began wondering about something curious regarding my practicing of the healing work from the Dolmen Arch. I've practiced it twice on Friend A and once on Friend B. Both of them experienced sensations of left/right imbalance during the work (Friend A only experienced this one of the two times).

Specifically, during the charging of the cauldrons Friend A felt their right side to be "heavy, thick, and enclosed" while their left side felt "naked." With Friend B the sensation occurred while I was preparing for the work - possibly even before I awakened my palm centers. Friend B felt their "energy body smooshed to their right side, leaving their left side empty." This sensation was "disconcerting," but ended for Friend B once the work proper started.

Both friends reported their experiences as overall favorable.

The data is yet slim, but I find myself wondering how these two might be connected, and how I might proceed. Specifically,

A) should I be worried about practicing the work on others, and
B) how might I correct my own energetic imbalances?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Personal imbalances affecting healing work with others
replies.content:
Oof! I'm glad to hear that you're recovering well, but that must have been an unpleasant experience. My sympathies!

I'm going to address your questions in reverse. The crucial thing to do for (B) is to keep working regularly with the self-healing practice. When you've finished each session, before you do the closing gesture, leave your palm centers pressed together for seven slow breaths, doing the palm center awakening exercise again. Concentrate on balancing the two sides of your body, the solar current flowing into the left side and the telluric current flowing into the right, through the joined palm centers.

As for (A), no, it shouldn't be any kind of problem.

replies.title: Re: Astrology, aspects to sign's ruler
replies.content:
These are all subtle factors; they do have an effect, but it's a modification to the basic meaning of Jupiter in Aries, say, or Saturn in Sagittarius. If you have a good astrology textbook -- I recommend Llewellyn George's A-Z Horoscope Maker and Delineator, the pre-1980 editions -- it should explain how each planet operates in each sign.

Remember also that the outer planets are generational influences, while the inner planets are personal. Some generations have more integration among the planetary forces than others.

comments.title: Political Magic
comments.content:
I have a question about an aspect of political magic that I haven't yet been able to make sense of. You frequently admonish that one should never perform any magic, even magic with a positive intention, towards another individual without their consent. This makes sense to me. But in light of that fact, what makes political magic okay? Politicians are people, and voters are people too, after all! Even when you attack the "magical resistance", it's normally on the basis of poor spellcraft and magical illiteracy, and the expected "raspberry jam" from performing hostile magic in general. What makes, for example, an unsolicited working for Donald Trump to lead the country with prudence and wisdom materially different from an unsolicited working that my best friend gets a promotion at work? Or magical workings to help a candidate get elected - can this be construed as manipulating the actions of others (i.e. how voters vote)?

As always, thank you for Magic Mondays!

replies.title: Re: Political Magic
replies.content: I don't advocate political magic at all, other than the sort of general blessing work that's appropriate in almost any context -- for example, a US citizen has every right to do candle work before an image of the Statue of Liberty with the intention that the United States should thrive and live up to its ideals, since the citizen in question is part of the nation.  The reason I don't criticize the moral aspect of political magic in my discussions of the soi-disant "Magic Resistance" is that it's wasted breath -- they've bought so completely into the narratives being pushed by the corporate media that they believe their cause is pure goodness and light and the other side is the evilest evil that ever eviled. To have any kind of effect on people who might otherwise tend to join in those misbegotten workings and get the karmic blowback from them, I need to address other issues.

comments.title: Godform assumption
comments.content:
1) Could you please explain in simple terms the purposes of godform assumption?

2) I am a young heterosexual guy who is considering Brigid worship. However, I have this slight worry that devotional practices to a female deity might make me too feminine. Any thoughts on that?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Godform assumption
replies.content: 2) From personal experience, I've had no problem with becoming too feminine from worshiping Brighid.

replies.title: Re: Godform assumption
replies.content:
1) There are two very different reasons to do godform assumption, one that I recommend in moderation, and another that I don't. The first is in initiatory ritual, where it's useful for the temple officers to mediate divine energies for the benefit of the candidate; taking on a godform makes it a lot easier to bring through the energies and direct them toward the candidate in the ritual. Then there's the practice of taking on godforms for personal spiritual transformation, which I don't recommend if you're doing Western magic. (There are some Asian spiritual traditions that do it and get good results, but they use their own methods and have various protections in place.) The people I've known personally who did a lot of that kind of godform work very often ended up with badly inflated egos, and that's too common in ceremonial magic as it is!

2) That's not something you have to worry about. You're not trying to become Brigid, but to enter into a polarity relationship with her, and because you're male and straight, that relationship is going to engage your sexual energies -- which is as it should be. (Pagan deities aren't squeamish about that.) Quite the contrary -- and with that robust a deity above all! -- devotional practices directed toward a goddess will energize the masculine aspects of your nature. That's why the Spartans -- who were, shall we say, not exactly the most feminine of Greeks! -- had their young men direct their worship to Artemis.

The shaggy creatures and the goat reminded me of ancient European folk ceremonies featuring people costumed similarly.

Here's an article about similar costumes:
https://bit.ly/2oZasy2

While watching, I thought these figures represented the wild, untamed spirits of the mountain. There were also a number of people in white costumes at one point, and I thought they represented ghosts. Ancestors, or spirits of people who died by the hand of the mountain?

Which is not to say it didn't have occult effects on the participants and audience (which apparently included heads of state -- there's a bit where they show members of the audience and I believe Angela Merkel was there). Somebody correct me if I'm wrong.

What does occur to me, occultly, is the question of whether or not the wild spirits of the mountain feel themselves to be adequately propitiated, and whether or not they appreciate the notion that they've been tamed or have come into some subordinate or even cooperative relationship with the tunnel and the people who built it.

comments.title: intervening in a bad situation (and karma?)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I did a divination to see if seeking counsel on a family member's situation would be a good idea and received a thumbs-up (I know that taking any advice given will need another round of divination, but at least I can start here).

My cousin married a woman who only after the marriage revealed herself to be conniving, spiteful and manipulative (once she got what she wanted, which was a big wedding, the status-bumper of the situation, and pregnant, and whatever else). She is an extreme narcissist and has grown up under a mother who is the same. The problem for my cousin is that the court awarded her majority custody of the now-2-year-old daughter. There are obvious biases in favor of women in custody battles and my cousin got hit with that. He gets to see his daughter 4 days per month and the rest of the time she's being raised in the other family's milieu.

His parents (my aunt and uncle) and he are capable, loving, generous and honest people and they want to raise the girl. This ongoing saga has cost them financially, emotionally,...personally.

I asked my uncle if he wished for me to look into "unconventional" methods to help find a solution and he said yes. They all understand raspberry jam and on their own have concluded that what needs to happen is not some sort of coercive maneuver, but for the mother to come to the realization that she is not fit to be the primary parent, that whether out of regard for the child's health, or out of a sense that parenting is too much of a burden and gets in the way of whatever other goals the mother has, she needs to either willingly concede custody to my cousin or her true nature needs to be made apparent in a way that the daughter is rightfully returned to her father.

I've not practiced any magic (the daily SOP/divination/meditation are where I'm at), so I'm no candidate for anything more than natural magic (and limited facility with prayer). My husband has strong ties to tantric (Tibetan) ritual practitioners in various parts of the Himalayas and they (obviously) have potent magical practices that could be marshalled. We want to be very careful here, though, to take the best approach (if we take any approach, that is - this is just a foray into thinking about it) - ultimately, we want the daughter to be in a safe, loving, nurturing home. We think it's with my cousin and his parents. FWIW, the child has said of the mother and her family, on more than one occasion, "I don't like those people" and there's evidence that their methods are affecting her (she gets hysterical when she's in a room with a closed door as she's kept in a room with a locked door at the other house).

Might you shed some light on a basic approach to this whole thing given the limitations we're working with, the lack of magical skill on my/our part, and ... our trying not to let the eye of Sauron turn our way?

This leads to a second question - for all I know, there could be really messed up (past life) karma between my cousin and the ex and the daughter... With our hope being to nudge things toward providing a better, healthier life for the child, how do we reconcile this with each soul's need to work through karma? How do we know we are working for good, in the end?

Thanks so much for any wisdom you can provide here.

replies.title: Re: intervening in a bad situation (and karma?)
replies.content:
Those tantric practitioners are the ones to talk to. If they're any good, and I assume they are, they know a good deal more about karma and its proper handling than I do, and can frame the entire process so that it works out for the benefit of all sentient beings. If your divinations indicate that you should go ahead, that's the direction I would suggest.

As for karma, you don't know -- indeed, you can't know -- what another person's karma is, so all you can do is act in accordance with your best judgment. For all you know, it's the daughter's karma to be rescued from an awkward and ugly situation by magic worked by a tantric practitioner in the Himalayas...

replies.title: Re: intervening in a bad situation (and karma?)
replies.content:
Oh! Bless your cousin. I hope things turn out well in the end for all parties.

From the practical side of things, went through similar with a relative not too long ago (he now has full custody).

Some (non-magical) things that might help, if he's not doing them already:

He needs to document EVERYTHING. Print out every email and text message. Make a note with time and date for every phone call. Save every letter. Put it all together in chronological order in a three-ring binder. Bonus points if he still has stuff from pre-divorce on his phone, or in his email archive. If she's on social media and you have access, document that (including photos). Hard copy. Every time daughter says something about ex or ex's family, write it down, date and time, file it. A lot of it will be irrelevant, but some might seem irrelevant until the next crazy thing you find out ("Oh, wait, those glitter shoes were a present from super-creepy friend Z" or "Oh, hey, that 'friend' just got arrested for Y"). You can go back later and use color-coded tabs to flag indicators of drug use (brush up on current slang if this is a concern: Urban Dictionary is helpful. I was previously not familiar with terms like "broccoli" and "shwasted"), alcohol abuse, contact with drug dealers, times when questionable people had access to the child, indicators of neglect or abuse, violence, threats, indicators of mental illness, any time child-support or other payments were handed over, etc. Lawyer will thank him.

If there is any sign of abuse or neglect, have the child evaluated by a reputable psychologist asap. Document the shale out of it.

If the ex is crazy enough, there will likely be concerned "friends", ex-friends, relatives, and acquaintances willing to talk to your cousin on the downlow if they know how to get in touch (in our case, he didn't even have to ask-- they volunteered, and kept him updated because they were concerned for the kids' safety). As much as I hate social media, this is one area where it is useful-- it makes you reach-able for relative strangers who are concerned about the welfare of your child. Best if profile pic is of your cousin and his kid smiling (so that if concerned relatives want to get in touch, they know they've got the right FB account), and no content that could be used against him. Post a cute cat meme and leave it at that ;)

Best of luck! Do you think he'd mind if I asked the Mother of God (nurturer of children) to look after the child?

replies.content:
"The Spiral Dance" is important historically, but it's something of a magical and Witchy pot-pourri. Starhawk writes somewhere that she first encountered Witchcraft when she happened upon Z Budapest's Witch-Shop, while she was working out her own blend of feminism and politics. I still see traces of Z's influence throughout the book. Later on she encountered other forms of Witchcraft and was influenced by them, too With this Witchcraft she also blended some New-Thought practices as they were current in California at the time: grounding and centering were New-Thought practices decades before Starhawk came on the scene, but it was she who brought them into American Witchcraft.

For me, the very best sentence in the entire book is a quote from Starhawk's mother, Bertha Simos, a psychiatrist, which serves as an epigraph to one chapter: "It is our limitations that keep us sane."

replies.title: early influences on Starhawk
replies.content:
Eyewitness reporter here. Z. Budapest founded the Susan B. Anthony Coven #1 in Los Angeles in the 1960s. Then she relocated to the San Francisco Bay Area and opened a store called The Feminist Wicca in Oakland, California.

By the time Z arrived (mid Seventies; I don't recall the year), there already was a thriving and varied neopagan scene in the SFBA. The largest and most public witchcraft traditions active in it were Victor and Cora Anderson's Feri Tradition and NROOGD (New Reformed Orthodox Order of the Golden Dawn) which began as an experiment and had multiple founding members. Neither trad was a direct knockoff of British Traditional Witchcraft. Both traditions had competent poets composing their liturgies. Both included men and women on an equal basis. NROOGD was pretty heteronormative in its early years, with the God secondary to the Goddess. Feri was more queer.

Feri trad (speaking as an outsider) focused on developing the psyche and cultivating altered states of consciousness. NROOGD's specialized lore concerns designing, organizing and leading large public sabbats that were symbolically and magically coherent and allowed untrained members of the public a chance to alter their consciousness safely. (Speaking as an initiate.)

The classic form of the NROOGD basic ritual ends with a thorough group grounding, something that participants enjoyed so much that I've been at sabbats where the leaders tried to skip it and the rest of the participants went ahead and did it anyway. The earliest form of the NROOGD ritual launched right into the Meeting Dance, but by the mid Seventies, it became customary to assemble all the participants in a circle and lead them in a brief (sixty seconds or so) individual grounding just before the ritual proper began.

NROOGD began as a bootstrap tradition, and the founders brought in whatever they knew about. There is almost nothing I recognize as New Thought in the NROOGD ethos and teachings--the invocation of the Threefold Goddess includes the words "Destroy us, Lady" and there is a lot of death and rebirth symbolism. But it is possible that the emphasis on grounding (not so much on centering) in NROOGD ritual came from New Thought at one or two removes.

Starhawk attended Feri and NROOGD rituals when she was still Miriam Simos, before she started writing The Spiral Dance.

replies.title: Re: early influences on Starhawk
replies.content:
Z started her shop, Feminist Wicca, in the LA area (in Santa Monica, IIRC), and she said that she and Starhawk first met in her LA shop sometime in the early 1970s. Somewhat later, as you said, Z moved up to the SF Bay area and took (a branch of?) her shop with her. I moved to Rhode Island in 1967, so I never met Z (or Starhawk, either), but I heard (at some remove in time and space) about a lot of what you witnessed firsthand.

Also, a lone teacher of BTW (as it is now called this side of the Atlantic) was already in the SF Bay area in 1961, but if my limited informartion is to be trusted) she was only there for a year or two, and then she returned to the UK. Aboutn the same time Margaret St. Clair and her husband seem to have started doing something rather like BTW in their home in the Berkeley Hills by 1963, before Ray Buckland got settled in on this side of the Atlantic. (This is probably where the forgotten roots of "Central Valley Wicca" lie.)

New Thought was a mighty presence in the SF Bay area (and in LA as well) in the early 20th century, along with many other occult and esoteric movements (many of which are now forgotten). As our host has said more than once, New Thought involves a lot of "magic with the serial numberts filed off," and I can document centering as a New-Thought practice in its publications works at least as far back as the early 1900s. So it would be natural enough for NROOGD to have picked it up from the Bay Area milieu, and Starhawk to have encountered it there, if indeed she had not already learned about it in some other Californian context before then.

replies.title: Spiral Dance--my initial take on it
replies.content:
When The Spiral Dance came out, I had been practicing witchcraft for a few years, been exposed to a couple of Craft traditions and been a member of a bootstrap coven.

I had a mixed reaction. This book was one of the first, if not the very first, books about witchcraft from an American witch that was not presented as journalism and was not dumbed down for the publisher's idea of who the readers would be. Starhawk got to present her ideas pretty much the way she wanted to. I liked that there was some attention to theology, not just spellcraft. I also thought that including exercises for the reader in each chapter was a good idea.

OTOH, her approach was too psychological for my taste. My initial approach to witchcraft as an adult had been to try to cultivate a relationship with the Goddess and do devotional work before anything else, and that entailed regarding Her as a being separate from myself.

The book also had a flavor that didn't seem very witchy to me. I put it down as all right in its way, but not my cup of tea, and never picked it up again except to consult the appendices, and that rarely.

The book apparently sold well, and that opened the door for other serious writers about contemporary witchcraft.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You don't assume the authority of a deity or an archangel, because you're not a deity or an archangel. Because you're a Christian, Jesus has granted you, as one of his children, the right to use the Name of Jesus to command the powers of the elements; look up "the name of Jesus" in your study Bible and see what it says. You use a different title of Jesus in each direction, as follows:

East/Air: the Word (John 1:1)
South/Fire: the Sun of Righteousness (Malachi 4:2)
West/Water: Emmanuel (Isaiah 7:14)
Earth/North: Son of David (Matthew 1:1)
Spirit Below: the Good Shepherd (John 10:11)
Spirit Above: the Lamb of God (John 1:29)
Spirit Within: the Resurrection and the Life (John 11:25)

So in the East, after tracing the invoking symbol, you might say: "In the Name of Jesus the Word, I invoke the Air, its angels, its spirits, and its powers, to guide and protect me." Trace the banishing symbol, and say: "And in the Name of Jesus that is above all other names, and with the help of the powers of air, I banish from within me and around me and from all my doings all harmful influences and hostile magic and every imbalance of the nature of Air. I banish them far from me." Change the title and the element for each of the subsequent directions.

Track your transits using your natal chart, and then check your relocation chart to see how they'll affect you where you live now.

2) A sweetening spell would likely be a good idea.

A while back, I asked about ways to deal with a particularly nasty love spell gone wrong story. Here it is in detail, so you have context for this.

I met someone about six years ago, who I found very attractive. I asked her out, and she rejected me, although we became good friends. She pursued a relationship with someone else, which I thought was committed, and about two years after meeting her I entered into a relationship with someone else.

A year or so later (three years ago), I unexpectedly had feelings for her pop up again. As I was working through an occult course, my first thought was karmic culmination. Deciding pursuing a relationship despite being into someone else was unfair, I broke up with my girlfriend. When the friend I had feelings for asked why, I explained I had feelings for her, and although I wasn't planning to act on them, it wasn't fair to date someone else if I couldn't be fully committed. At about the same time, her relationship fell apart completely, although I never did find out exactly what happened.

She then spent most of the next year ignoring me, and telling people increasingly horrible things about me to justify this reaction. Eventually, she decided she wanted to talk again, and I decided not to listen: our friendship was gone and nothing she said would convince me to want it back, nor to ever trust her again: we had considered each other best friends, and she threw it away.

She's had a string of unstable relationships since, and has complained to someone that she thinks I cursed her, since she's never been able to have a relationship with anyone who's not like me. I'm not sure what she means by like me, but I'm extremely sensitive and have attempted suicide twice before, and so this seems like it could be very, very bad.

1) What options do I have to protect others from the consequences of this spell? I have a decent grasp of Golden Dawn magic, but I'm unsure of what options I have short of binding her not to have a relationship.

My divination about doing nothing indicated at least one person will die, and so this is not something I want to let happen.

2) If I cast a binding, I also assume a cut and clear spell will not be an option, as it would also sever the link which I used to bind her. Is this a fair assessment?

comments.title: Meditation
comments.content:
After 7 weeks of regular (5 days a week) meditation, a few questions have arisen. The usual scheme is relaxation, cleansing breath, yellow color breathing, meditation, cleansing breath, at times a short prayer at the beginning and/or end. When I am tired beyond a certain level, the whole thing becomes rather exhausting - especially during color breathing concentration is very hard to maintain, the body hurts, etc. When I'm not too tired it usually works nicely and most of the times I have to finish when the alarm clock rings after 20 to 30 minutes and I have to go on doing other things. So much as an intro - here are my questions:

1) Is it possible to replace a certain of your sleep with meditation? If not too tired in advance, I feel rather refreshed after meditation, almost as if I had slept an equal amount of time.

2) When I concentrate on the blackness behind my closed eyes, sometimes a weird sensation arises. It's hard to describe. It feels as if my head is bigger than usual and the rest of my body much smaller, far below me. It's not really pleasant. If I move a body part or open my eyes, my body feeling is normal again. Can you comment anything on that?

3) I've kept the discursive part low so far. I started stabilizing the "technical" part, calming my mind and getting routine. I've done some receptive work, and some work on personal issues. I feel, that there's a lot to discover and understand that way. Distant memories and how they shape me. Yesterday something that could be a dim past-live memory showed up. It was accompanied by a rather intense body reaction - I went warm and started to sweat, breath got deeper and heavier - as if you are under tension. I have to admit, this kind of working fascinates and scares me at the same time. It reminds me of experiences with methods of psychotherapy on the occult end of the spectrum. Do you have any advice? Any risks, safety precautions, etc.?

Thank you very much!
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Meditation
replies.content:
1) Some people find that this works well for them.

2) Hmm! I'm not familiar with that. You can meditate with your eyes open, for what it's worth.

3) If you start getting strong emotional reactions or body reactions, you might want to put a few days into meditating on a less personal theme, just to give yourself the time to recover. Other than that, discursive meditation is very safe and you shouldn't have any trouble.

I'd like to start practicing some magical workings (beginning with the SoP), but I can't manage to get myself off the fence. I've heard enough stories of spells going bad, or loopholes with spirits that weren't properly closed, or naive magicians who ended up an unwitting plaything of the gods that I always lose my nerve. The fact of the matter is, I'm afraid to do anything because I'm too inexperienced in all things magical, but I'm too inexperienced in all things magical because I'm afraid to do anything. Even the invocation of the gods/spirits/powers of the elements in the SoP makes me nervous. Do you have any advice for an anxious practitioner?

On a lighter note, a couple theoreticals I'm curious about:

1) It seems a common practice with occultists to petition Christian saints and angels, and use often extremely religious prayers to the Christian god in different magical workings. It strikes me as odd that these powers aren't horribly offended by having their services requested by people outside the faith who are engaging in practices the church has long condemned. Any theories as to why this might be?

2) Do you think the gods can change domain and function as their cultus evolves? For instance, Cernunnos can be convincingly argued to have been a god of wealth and liminality in ancient Gaul, but the majority of his modern devotees see him as a sort of Pan-figure - a Lord of the Animals or forest god - despite questionable evidence. Nonetheless, they seem to get solid results from their prayers and workings to him. Is it possible that Cernunnos shifted roles to better meet the expectations of his modern followers, or is it more likely that these worshipers are actually contacting someone else entirely?

Thanks as always for doing these Q&As!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
0) Fear, as a once-famous book puts it, is the first enemy of a man of knowledge. You and only you can decide whether you will push past the fear and do the thing anyway.

1) The Christian god seems to be a lot more tolerant than most of his followers are. Of course there are plenty of Christian occultists -- the fact that certain churches get bent out of shape about magic is a matter of the history of religious bureaucracy, not of the history of actual practice.

2) Gods change domain and function all the time. That's not a new thing, either -- according to some studies, Apollo started out as a plague god associated with mice, and clawed his way up the divine ladder from there.

comments.title: Discursive meditation
comments.content:
Good evening JMG,

I had an odd experience while doing the spiritus meditation out of APG this morning; one of the thoughts which distracted me from focus on the breath was a distressing memory of a fairly sharp defeat from several years ago -- the memory was vivid enough that I internally winced, clenched my jaw, etc, before managing to bring awareness back to the rhythm of breath. Not five breaths later, the exact same memory surfaced again, only this time there was no emotional involvement whatsoever -- no grimace, no nothing, just continued attempts to bring awareness back to the breath while the memory came and went. This seems absurd on several levels: literally nothing about what happened had (or has) changed, the recollection was the same as usual, but the emotive response could not have been more different. Why? Seemingly nothing whatsoever about the stimulus was changed, yet the response was completely changed. Am I correct in assuming this is the entire point of the enterprise? It certainly feels almost like a superpower, especially if it could be harnessed, controlled, and deployed at will in one's daily life.

Very odd.

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditation
replies.content: I wouldn't call it a superpower, but it's very powerful -- and yes, the ability to release stuck emotions is one of the points of the enterprise.

comments.title: Anxieties
comments.content:
Archdruid,

Last week I asked about my anxieties which are suddenly boiling to the surface because I did a working to stoke my fiery strength of Will. You advised that I meditate upon and journal the anxieties that I'm feeling, and I've spent the last week following your advice. My conclusions, which are closely related to all the meditations I did about the Will's relationship to fire, are that I have a lot of work to do. The anxieties and self-doubt I have stem from a life time of lack of confidence, self-worth, fear of failure, and investment anxiety.

Like one of your readers last week, I too have a habit of imagining myself performing amazing feats in front of friends and strangers. I've used that habit consistently to make up for my perceived failures and lack of initiative.

Even when I accomplish amazing tasks, I never accept them and consistently berate myself for imperfections. The thought always being "why can't you be like that guy you constantly imagine?"

I've been terrified of failure my whole life, because I've been told how smart and talented my whole life. The beginning of any skill comes very easily to me, but to progress to mastery becomes more challenging than I can manage. My passion wanes as the hill becomes steeper.

I'm always so anxious about what I haven't accomplished and what I have yet to accomplish that I never dedicate myself 100% to anything, and when I try my anxieties rise like a wind to scatter the heat of my passion.

Each of these barriers to Will are reflective of a polluted elemental form. The lack of confidence to fire, the lack of self-worth to earth, the fear of failure to water, and investment anxiety to air.

I think this is a start. I need to meditate and journal further to understand how to deal with these.

It seems my Will is more inhibited than I thought, but that I'm closer to resolving those inhibitions than I thought.

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: Anxieties
replies.content: Excellent! Knowing what you have to do is more than half the battle. Proceed as you've begun and you'll rise out of all those anxieties and troubles.

comments.title: Steiner
comments.content:
Hello JMG,
Long time listener, first time caller 😊

I’m in the process of doing some research into Waldorf schools, as my 3 year old will be starting preschool soon, and since I have not read Steiner at all I’m trying to get an understanding on his basic philosophy and anthroposophy.
The online world seems to be either very anti Steiner (he’s a racist) or completely in love with him (he’s an unsurpassed visionary) So I decided to come to a place I can always trust to be sane and rational...
I fell into Waldorf because I appreciate the minimal technology and emphasis on art, crafts and outside play but I do want to understand the ideas and philosophy behind it all - can you share a little knowledge, maybe point me in the right direction?
Much appreciated
✨

replies.title: Re: Steiner
replies.content: The Waldorf system is one of Steiner's better creations; if I'd had the chance to raise a child or two and we could have afforded it, a Waldorf school would have been my first choice. Steiner's philosophy isn't easy to get into -- nobody's yet done a "Steiner for Beginners" book, as far as I know -- but it's one version of standard late 19th/early 20th century occult philosophy, colored by Steiner's background in mainstream philosophy and by his insistence on personal freedom as a basic principle. You might find this web page useful as an intro to his basic values -- figure out what a teacher teaches to beginning students and you've got a good handle on his personality!

replies.title: Re: Steiner--Waldorf schools
replies.content:
Two of my grandson's went to a local Waldorf school with mixed results. It was a small school and there was no central direction--each teacher ran classes in relative independence and, as you may know, the system keeps students with the same teacher for entire k-6 or 8 schooling. Great if the child works well with that teacher, not so good if they are not a good fit. The schools delay the teaching of reading until 3rd grade as part of their theory of child development. But my youngest grandson really wanted to learn to read and was disappointed that his teacher did not give real instruction even in 3rd grade. She seemed to feel that the students would just pick it up Summerhill style. He transferred to public school not even reading at Dr. Seus "Hop on Pop" level and was reading Lemony Snicket (which is 5-8 grade level) by spring--so you can see it was not any lack of ability on his part.

Some Waldorf people have a really hard time adapting to changing times and circumstances--one school for training Waldorf teachers lost access to Federal student loans due to problems getting the faculty and administration to adapt grading standards and course credits to match the requirements. Okay, aging hippies don't want to deal with The Man, but if you want money from Uncle Sugar you have to play by Uncle Sugar's rules--that is Real Life 101.

OTH, a friend of mine has her daughter in one of the Waldorf style public schools that are available in parts of California and she is very happy with the result. I would say visit a number of different schools, Waldorf and Montessori and perhaps non-affiliated schools of various kinds. Watch, interview, read. Do be aware of low vaccination rates at some private schools, if that is a concern for you. As far as Steiner's theories about race--I don't think they percolate down to the level of the kids or are taken seriously by current staff.
